clean_story:
Warm Pacific waters. Global weather disrupter.

The name El Niño—Spanish for "the boy"—comes from Peruvian fishermen who noticed unusually warm coastal waters around Christmas, linking it to the Christ child. It’s the Earth’s way of hiccuping heat.

Normally, trade winds push warm surface water west, letting cooler water rise near South America. But every 2–7 years, these winds weaken. The warm water sloshes back east like a bathtub left unstirred, smothering the cool upwelling. Fish vanish; rain follows the heat. Indonesia dries into drought; Peru floods. The shift is subtle—a few degrees Celsius in ocean temperature—but its ripple reshapes jet streams worldwide. In 1997, El Niño fueled fires in Brazil, mudslides in California, and a record $96 billion in global damage.

Satellites now track its early signs: a bulge of warm water creeping across the Pacific, measured in millimeters of sea-level rise. Yet predicting its strength remains imperfect, like guessing a storm’s path by watching distant clouds.

Three related subjects are ocean current engines, monsoon failure patterns, and how butterflies alter storms.
